the real trick is finding the “breed” you’re most similar to and designing your environment around it instead of coping relentlessly

text: I have 100k followers across platforms and have experienced remarkably few of the negative side effects of Minor Notoriety. This is because i share almost no details of consequences about myself and never will.
thread: I have 100k followers across platforms and have experienced remarkably few of the negative side effects of Minor Notoriety. This is because i share almost no details of consequences about myself and never will.

I would recommend this for any content creator btw. The internet is not a place of self expression. You will stay far more safe and sane not telling people what ur feeling/ all the minor events in your life

Just don’t make it easy for people to *feel* like they know you, however untrue that may actually be 

This is bad for growth but good for mental health
reply:
yeah this is probably the sanest take, i think what people underestimate is just how much people *want* to feel like they know you, and will fill in any gaps with projection or fantasy regardless

the only real question is whether your sanity or your growth is more precious to you at any given time, but tbh the people who manage to grow *without* oversharing are playing the game on easy mode

text:
I don't believe in intuitive eating because the delta between nutritional needs and actual appetite can vary SO massively. 

Yes, after tracking my intake for a couple months and eating exactly what I need, my appetite usually adjusts to that and I can eat somewhat "intuitively"
thread:
I don't believe in intuitive eating because the delta between nutritional needs and actual appetite can vary SO massively. 

Yes, after tracking my intake for a couple months and eating exactly what I need, my appetite usually adjusts to that and I can eat somewhat "intuitively"

But people eat for lots of reasons that are not hunger, most commonly boredom and anxiety. In my experience appetite is somewhat elastic. 

After even a few days of recreational eating, say a holiday, my appetite stretches to match my intake.

usually the difference is just 100-300 calories every day, but that's enough to put on quite a lot of weight over time

you can trust your body only if your body is ALREADY USED to what is good for you
reply:
yeah totally, your point about “you can only trust your body if it’s already calibrated” is so key.  
people act like their random cravings are wisdom when it’s literally just dopamine circuits + habit. 

appetite for most of us is downstream of environment, baseline routines, and what we reinforce… not some magical intuition.
